entry.title: Redesigning Work for Human–AI Collaboration – An OCM Blueprint
entry.content:
“AI is live” is not the same as “AI is delivering value.” The difference usually lives in the workflow. If roles, steps, and decisions haven’t changed, you’re probably running pre‑AI processes on top of AI tools. Value doesn’t come from turning features on. It comes from redesigning how humans and AI collaborate.

 

When “AI Is Live” Still Feels Like “Before”

Many organisations have now had their first AI programme experience. The pattern is familiar:

  • The model goes live.

  • Training sessions happen.

  • Dashboards show promising adoption numbers.

Yet when you look at cost, quality, cycle time, or risk, the improvement is modest at best.

Dig into the day‑to‑day work and a consistent theme emerges: the workflow never really changed. AI is present in the process, but the process itself is still designed for a pre‑AI world.

OCM’s job is to help teams do the harder work: redesigning how work flows so AI capability and human capability actually combine.

 

Why Workflow Redesign Matters Now

Previous waves of automation mainly targeted individual steps. A manual action was replaced; a recurring task was removed.

AI changes something deeper. It introduces prediction, summarisation, classification, and recommendation at multiple points. That shifts the logic of the whole process:

  • Bottlenecks move and new ones emerge.

  • Human‑only tasks become hybrid.

  • Role boundaries change even when job titles stay the same.

  • The “right” sequence of steps is different when you can anticipate issues or generate outputs early.

If you simply plug AI into the legacy flow without redesigning, people will work around the system and the value will stay theoretical.

 

A Four‑Step Blueprint for Human–AI Workflow Design

You don’t need a complex methodology to start doing this well. You need a simple blueprint and the discipline to apply it consistently.

1. Map the AI Touchpoints

First, get concrete. Instead of talking about “AI in finance” or “AI in customer service”, identify exact touchpoints:

  • Where does AI generate content or summaries?

  • Where does it classify, prioritise, triage, detect anomalies, or recommend actions?

  • Which artefacts (cases, orders, invoices, tickets) and which decisions are affected?

Write these down visually. Many teams discover that they haven’t fully mapped where AI is actually entering the process.

2. Clarify What Humans Do Now

For each touchpoint, define the human role in the new workflow:

  • Are they reviewing AI outputs?

  • Making final decisions?

  • Correcting errors or adding context?

  • Escalating edge cases?

  • “Teaching” the system through structured feedback?

Update RACI and handoffs to reflect hybrid tasks. Document who is accountable, who is responsible, who must be consulted, and who is informed when AI is in the loop.

Without this clarity, you get blind trust (“the system is always right”) or blanket distrust (“I ignore the system and do it myself”), and both damage value. Human accountability doesn’t disappear in AI workflows. It just becomes easier to blur if you don’t design for it.

 

3. Decide What Must Stay Human

Just because AI can influence a decision doesn’t mean it should own it.

Work with risk, compliance, and business stakeholders to define:

  • Decisions that are always human, regardless of model confidence.

  • Decisions where AI can propose but humans must sign off.

  • Low‑risk decisions that can be auto‑handled under defined thresholds.

Document human‑in‑the‑loop patterns like:

  • Approve‑before‑act – AI proposes, human approves, then the action occurs.

  • Approve‑after‑act – AI acts, humans review samples or exceptions.

  • Review‑on‑exception – humans only touch anomalies and risk‑tiered cases.

Set clear thresholds for auto‑accept and auto‑escalate. This is where explainability and confidence measures matter.

4. Build Frontline Feedback Loops

Assume your first version of the redesigned workflow will be imperfect. The question is whether you’ll learn from it quickly.

Design simple ways for frontline staff to report:

  • Quality issues – where AI outputs are consistently off.

  • Cognitive overload – steps where AI adds complexity.

  • Handoff problems – confusion about who owns a case or decision.

Establish review cadences with named owners. Close the loop: when feedback leads to changes in models or steps, communicate those changes back to the teams.

Apply this blueprint early in the programme and revisit it regularly. Don’t let workflow design freeze after go‑live.

 

Operating Model Redesign: The Hidden Layer

Behind every workflow sits an operating model: who owns processes, who makes decisions, how performance is measured.

AI changes these structures. Information becomes more widely available. Some decisions can be made closer to the frontline. Others move up because risk is higher. Some teams find their workload redistributed.

If you don’t make these changes explicit:

  • Authority and workload can shift in ways nobody has agreed.

  • Shadow decision‑makers emerge.

  • Tensions build as power dynamics change.

OCM’s role is to surface and manage this layer:

  • Make changes to ownership, decision rights, and accountabilities visible and communicated.

  • Involve stakeholders whose authority or workload shifts.

  • Help leaders anticipate cultural and power implications of the new design.

 

Where SAP Signavio and SAP Activate Support the Blueprint

In SAP‑enabled environments, two assets are particularly helpful.

  • SAP Signavio lets you model current processes, simulate AI‑driven changes, and connect outcomes to SAP execution reality. You can see where AI capabilities would sit, how they alter flows, and what impact they have on cycle times and handoffs.apty

  • SAP Activate includes an OCM workstream that brings people and process considerations into the project from Discover to Run, rather than bolting them on at the end.prosci

Use these tools as collaboration environments. Bring process owners, IT, and frontline representatives together to co‑design human–AI workflows. Don’t treat them as static documentation repositories.

 

A Concrete Scenario: Invoice Processing

Take invoice processing as an example.

Pre‑AI, the process might involve:

  • Manual capture and validation of invoice data.

  • Rule‑based checks for duplicates, coding, and thresholds.

  • Exceptions handled by experienced staff.

  • Approvals mainly driven by static limits.

Post‑AI, you may have:

  • Automated capture and validation.

  • Anomaly detection flagging unusual patterns.

  • Recommendation engines proposing approvals, routes, and actions.

Apply the blueprint:

  • Define anomaly classes that always require human review vs. those that can be auto‑handled, with confidence thresholds (for example, “over 95% confidence, under €X, auto‑approve”).

  • Clarify that analysts now focus on investigating flagged patterns rather than repeating machine checks.

  • Establish escalation routes and time‑to‑decision targets for ambiguous cases.

  • Feed false positives and negatives into model and rule refinement.

The result: faster cycle times, preserved or improved risk control, and clearer accountability – not just “AI in invoices”.

 

Design Assets and KPIs That Make It Stick

To keep this grounded, OCM can help deliver:

  • Human–AI RACI maps per process.

  • Decision matrices by risk tier and confidence threshold.

  • Escalation maps and response SLAs.

  • Exception‑handling playbooks.

  • Embedded guidance copy for SAP UX.

  • KPI packs tracking throughput, queue depth, exception rates, override patterns, rework, cognitive load signals, and model performance.

These assets let you see and manage the human–AI collaboration, not just the system status.

 

Pick One Workflow and Redesign It With AI in Mind

If AI is live but value feels elusive, don’t start with the tech. Start with a workflow.

Call to action:
Choose one end‑to‑end process where AI is already in play. Run the four‑step blueprint with the people who live that process every day – and let what you learn there guide the rest of your AI portfolio.

entry.title: Split authorization for posting and reversing supplier invoice
entry.content:
Hi,

Is it possible in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to allow users to post supplier invoices but prevent them from reversing supplier invoices?

I have been looking into the authorization settings, but it seems that the authorization for posting and reversing supplier invoices cannot be separated.

Could you please confirm whether this is standard system behavior? Is there any workaround?

entry.title: Re: Understanding Maintenance Event Builder (MEB) Through an Aerospace Maintenance Scenario
entry.content:
Hi @SNANDA 

Thank you for your feedback! I am glad you found the SAP snapshot helpful. You're absolutely right, MEB provides a comprehensive view of maintenance planning, streamline complex maintenance processes, especially in industries like aerospace & defense.

entry.title: How to Migrate Variants and Bookmarks to Multidimensional Analysis in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
1. Introduction — Don't Let Your Saved Work Get Left Behind

Start with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public CE2608 Edition, customers can complete a straightforward technical upgrade when migrating from classic Web Dynpro Grid reporting apps to modern Multidimensional Analysis (MDA) applications. However, one critical detail is frequently overlooked until the last moment: all personalized variants and bookmarks created by business users within Web Dynpro Grid apps, which have been accumulated over months or years of daily operations.

Think about your finance team's month-end close report: opened in the Web Dynpro Grid app, filters set to exactly the right company codes, fiscal year variant, and column layout, then saved as a bookmark directly inside that Web Dynpro Grid application and reused every single month. Or the controller who spent an afternoon inside a Web Dynpro Grid report fine-tuning a personal view with their preferred filter combinations — saved as a variant within that same Web Dynpro Grid app. These personalized variants and bookmarks are not just convenience features. They represent real working time invested by real users.

When you transition users to the new MDA application, their saved personalized content will not carry over automatically. All variants and bookmarks reside within the legacy Web Dynpro Grid application and will remain there unless there is a dedicated migration step.

The good news: you do not have to recreate any of it manually.

SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition provides a dedicated migration tool — the Migrate Variants and Bookmarks app (App ID: F7001) — that transfers all variants and bookmarks created in your Web Dynpro Grid applications to the corresponding MDA applications automatically, in a single background job.

This blog post walks you through everything you need to know: what gets migrated, how the process works step by step, what to watch out for, and how to handle edge cases. Whether you are a Key User, an SAP consultant, or a project manager planning an analytics modernization, this guide gives you the complete picture.

2. What Are Variants and Bookmarks?

Before diving into the migration process, it is worth understanding exactly what we are migrating — because the two concepts are related but distinct, and both are created and stored inside the Web Dynpro Grid application.

Variants are saved filter settings. When a user opens a Web Dynpro Grid report and sets filters — for example, selecting a specific company code, a date range, and a currency — they can save those settings as a named variant within that Web Dynpro Grid app. The next time they open the app, they simply select their saved variant and all filters are applied instantly. Variants save time and ensure consistency, especially for reports run on a recurring basis.

Bookmarks go one step further. It typically preserves the report layout, with the option to save filter setting as needed. Users are able to rapidly alter report layouts by switching between bookmarks.

There are two types of bookmarks, and both are fully supported by the migration:

  • Bookmark with filter values — the bookmark captures both the report layout and the specific filter values the user had set at the time of saving. When this bookmark is opened in MDA after migration, the same filters are pre-applied.
  • Bookmark without filter values — the bookmark captures the report layout and navigation state only, without locking in any specific filter values. The user is prompted to enter filter values when they open it. This type is equally supported and migrates successfully.

Both variants and bookmarks created in Web Dynpro Grid apps support three visibility levels:

Visibility Level

Who Can See It

Private

Creator Only

Public

All users of the application

Group

Specific user group members

Important — how MDA handles this differently:  At the time of this writing, the new MDA applications do not have a dedicated "bookmark" concept. 

In MDA, everything is merged into one unified concept: the app variant. The migration handles this conversion automatically.

Note on visibility: Group visibility is not supported in MDA. Any group variants or group bookmarks will be migrated to public app variants in MDA.

3. Understanding the Migration Architecture

The migration works because SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition maintains a successor relationship between each Web Dynpro Grid application and its corresponding MDA application. This relationship is the backbone of the entire process — it is the mechanism the system uses to know exactly where to send your variants and bookmarks when you run the migration.

Here is the end-to-end picture of how everything connects:

┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│     Web Dynpro Grid App         │
│  (variants + bookmarks stored   │
│   here, created by users)       │
└────────────┬────────────────────┘
             │
             │  reference relationship
             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────┐        ┌──────────────────────────────┐
│  Analytical Query V1            │──────► │  Analytical Query V3         │
│  (original query used by WDGA)  │migrate │  (new query used by MDA)     │
└─────────────────────────────────┘        └───────────────┬──────────────┘
                                                           │  InA Service
                                                           ▼
                                           ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                                           │     MDA Application          │
                                           │  (successor app on Fiori     │
                                           │   Launchpad)                 │
                                           └───────────────┬──────────────┘
                                                           │
                                                           │  F7001 migration job
                                                           ▼
                                           ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                                           │  Migrated App Variants       │
                                           │  (all your variants +        │
                                           │   bookmarks, now in MDA)     │
                                           └──────────────────────────────┘

4. Two Scenarios — Which One Applies to You?

The steps you need to complete depends on the  Web Dynpro Grid app is a  standard SAP delivery or a custom app created by a Key User.

Scenario

App Creator

Steps Required

SAP-delivered Web Dynpro Grid app

SAP (delivered as part of S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition)

Step 3 only — SAP has already migrated the underlying query and created the successor MDA app during the system upgrade

Custom Web Dynpro Grid app

A Key User, created via Manage KPIs and Reports or View Browser

All 3 steps — you perform the query migration and MDA creation yourself, then run the variant/bookmark migration

System Landscape Note

The step you are in determines where you do the work:

  • 3-system landscape (Development → Quality → Production): Key user performs steps 1 and 2 in your Development system. Transport the created artifacts (migrated query, MDA app) through your regular transport process to Quality and then Production. Run Step 3 — the variant/bookmark migration — independently in each system where your users need their variants and bookmarks available.
  • 2-system landscape (Quality → Production): Key user performs Steps 1 and 2 in your Quality system, transport to Production, then run Step 3 in each system.

5. Step-by-Step Migration Guide

Apps Involved in This Process

Before starting, here is a quick reference of all the Fiori apps you will use across the three steps:

App Name App ID Role in the Migration

App Name

App ID

Step in the Migration

Custom Analytical Queries and Services

F1572A

Step 1 — Migrates the report query from V1 to V3 and creates the InA Service

Manage KPIs and Reports

F2814

Step 2 — Converts the Web Dynpro Grid app to a new MDA app

Custom Catalog Extensions

F1950

Step 2 — Publishes the new MDA app to the Fiori Launchpad

Migrate Variants and Bookmarks

F7001

Step 3 — Transfers all variants and bookmarks created in WDGA to the MDA app

Application Job Log

F7002

Step 3 — Shows the detailed results of the migration background job

Step 1 — Migrate the Analytical Query from V1 to V3

App: Custom Analytical Queries and Services (F1572A)
Performer: Key User with Analytics Specialist role(SAP standard business role is SAP_BR_ANALYTICS_SPECIALIST)
System: Development (3-system landscape) or Quality (2-system landscape)

The Web Dynpro Grid application runs on an analytical query of version 1 (V1). The new MDA application requires an analytical query of version 3 (V3). This step upgrades the underlying query and creates the InA Service that exposes the V3 query for use by the MDA application.

This step is what establishes the successor relationship in the system — the invisible link that later tells the F7001 migration job which variants and bookmarks belong to which MDA app.

Actions:

  1. Open the Custom Analytical Queries and Services app (F1572A) from the SAP Fiori Launchpad.
  2. Find the analytical query that your Web Dynpro Grid application currently uses.
  3. Select the query and choose Migrate to generate the updated V3 version of the query, refer to this KBA 3603185  for details.
  4. After the migration completes, choose Create InA Service to make the V3 query available for the new MDA application.

Why this matters for variant migration: The V3 query migration preserves the technical name link back to the original V1 query. This link is exactly what the F7001 app uses in Step 3 to identify which variants and bookmarks — created in the old Web Dynpro Grid app — belong to the new MDA application. Without this link, the variant migration cannot work.

Step 2 — Convert the Web Dynpro Grid App to MDA

App: Manage KPIs and Reports (F2814) + Custom Catalog Extensions (F1950)
Performer: Key User with Analytics Specialist role
System: Development (3-system landscape) or Quality (2-system landscape)

Now that the V3 query exists, this step creates the actual MDA application and makes it available to users on the Fiori Launchpad. The Copy and Convert function in the Manage KPIs and Reports app reads the existing Web Dynpro Grid app configuration and generates the corresponding MDA app based on the V3 query.

What to do:

  1. Open the Manage KPIs and Reports app (F2814) from the SAP Fiori Launchpad.
  2. Locate the Web Dynpro Grid application you are replacing.
  3. Select it and choose the Copy and Convert button.
  4. Follow the guided prompts to create the new MDA application.
  5. Once the MDA application is created, open the Custom Catalog Extensions app (F1950).
  6. Publish the new MDA application to make it visible on the SAP Fiori Launchpad for your business users.

What's missing at this point: The new MDA application is now live on the Launchpad — but it does not yet contain any of the variants and bookmarks that users created in the old Web Dynpro Grid app. Those are still stored in the old app. Step 3 is what moves them across.

Step 3 — Transfer Variants and Bookmarks to MDA

App: Migrate Variants and Bookmarks (F7001)
Performer: Key User with Analytics Specialist role
System: Each system where users need access to their migrated variants and bookmarks (Development, Quality, and/or Production)

This is the step that delivers the actual value: all variants and bookmarks that users created inside the Web Dynpro Grid applications are transferred to their corresponding MDA applications if exists — in a single background job run, covering all eligible apps in the system at once. Users do not need to do anything themselves.

Required Authorization:

To run the F7001 app, your user needs one of the following:

  • Business catalog: BC_SAP_CA_BC_SSB
  • Application authorization variants: F7001_TRAN, F7002_TRAN, and F9021_TRAN

This authorization is typically included in the SAP standard SAP_BR_ANALYTICS_SPECIALIST business role or any your equivalent business role that include the above business catalog or application authorization variants.

What to do:

  1. Open the Migrate Variants and Bookmarks app (F7001) from the SAP Fiori Launchpad.
  2. Choose Migrate to start the migration.
  3. The system immediately schedules and runs a background job. You do not need to stay in the app — you can navigate away or close the browser.
  4. To check progress, return to F7001 and choose Refresh in the message strip to check if job is finished or not.
  5. When the background job finishes, you automatically receive a notification on your Fiori Launchpad homepage. Select the notification to return directly to F7001 and see the result.
  6. Choose Display Log to open the Application Job Log (F7002), where you can review a detailed record of every variant and bookmark that was processed.
  7. Open the new MDA application and spot-check that your variants and bookmarks appear correctly.

Tip: Run the migration in your Development or Quality system first to verify the results before running it in Production. The job is fully re-runnable.

 
Trigger Migration:
Migration job finish notification:
Click the Refresh button in the message bar above. Once the job is completed, the Migrate button will become clickable.

Click Display Log from F7001 to review the migration log.

6. Application Mapping Scenarios

Not all Web Dynpro Grid to MDA relationships are simple one-to-one mappings. The following table covers all supported scenarios so you know what to expect in your specific system setup:

Mapping Scenario What Happens During Migration

Mapping Scenario

What Happens During Migration

One Web Dynpro Grid app → One MDA app

The most common case. All variants and bookmarks created in the Web Dynpro Grid app migrate to the single successor MDA app.

One Web Dynpro Grid app → Multiple MDA apps

If two MDA apps were created based on the same underlying query, variants and bookmarks from the Web Dynpro Grid app are migrated to both MDA apps. Users see their saved work in either app they open.

Multiple Web Dynpro Grid apps → One MDA app

If multiple Web Dynpro Grid apps share the same underlying query, all their variants and bookmarks are migrated to the single MDA app. All migrated items are visible when the MDA app is opened, regardless of which Web Dynpro Grid app they originally came from.

Web Dynpro Grid app without corresponding successor MDA app

If a Web Dynpro Grid app has variants and bookmarks but no successor MDA app exists in the system, those items are not migrated and will not appear in the F7001 app. 

Practical implication of the N:1 scenario: If your organization used Save as Tile extensively to create personalized Web Dynpro Grid tiles for different teams or cost centers, all those teams' variants and bookmarks will be visible together in the single MDA app after migration. 

7. What Gets Migrated — and What Doesn't

What Transfers Successfully

Everything listed below is migrated automatically from your Web Dynpro Grid applications to the MDA apps:

What You Had in the Web Dynpro Grid App What You Get in the New MDA App

What You Had in the Web Dynpro Grid App

What You Get in the New MDA App

Variants (saved filter settings)

App variants(filter values)

Bookmarks with filter values

App variants (filter values + report layout)

Bookmarks without filter values

App variants (report layout)

Private variants / bookmarks

Private app variants (visible only to the original creator)

Public variants / bookmarks

Public app variants (visible to all users of the MDA app)

Group variants / bookmarks

Public app variants (group visibility does not exist in MDA)

What Does NOT Transfer — and What Users Should Do

Not everything from a Web Dynpro Grid variant or bookmark can be represented in MDA. The following known limitations apply:

What Is Not Migrated Impact What Users Should Do After Migration

What Is Not Migrated

Impact

What Users Should Do After Migration

Chart type and axis configuration

Chart settings (chart type, axis layout) are specific to the Web Dynpro Grid viewer and are not carried over

Open the MDA app, configure the preferred chart view, and save a new app variant in MDA app

Suppress Zeros setting

Zero suppression at the grid or axis level may not be fully preserved after migration

Re-enable the Suppress Zeros setting manually in the MDA application

Default variant flag

The "set as default" setting for a variant is not migrated

After migration, open the MDA app and set the preferred variant as default

"Contains" / "Not contains" operators in variants / bookmarks

These filter operators are removed from variants during migration to prevent errors in MDA, as MDA does not support these types of operators.

Recreate the filter using supported operators

Recommendation: Prior to go-live, prepare a brief communication to inform business users of these limitations. Minor adjustments will be necessary after migration. Coordinate with business users ahead of time to avoid misunderstandings. 

8. Running the Migration Again

A key point regarding the F7001 migration is that the tool supports repeated execution, so a rigid cutover date is not required. Users can keep working in the old Web Dynpro Grid app while the MDA rollout is in progress.

Re-execution Timing Rules :

  • Any bookmarks created or modified after the last migration run will be automatically included in the subsequent execution.
  • Variants created or modified after the last migration run will not be captured on the same day. Due to a technical constraint, they will be included beginning the next day.

Duplicate handling: If a variant or bookmark with the same name already exists in MDA, the system does not overwrite it. Instead, the newly migrated item will be added as a separate entry alongside the existing one. Users may delete the older version directly from within the MDA application.

Critical warning — Do not delete the Web Dynpro Grid application prior to migration. If you remove a Web Dynpro Grid app before variant and bookmark migration finishes, the variants and bookmarks stored within it cannot be recovered or migrated later.Deprecating or unpublishing the application is safe. Deleting it ahead of migration will result in permanent, irreversible data loss. 

9. Conclusion and Next Steps

Moving from Web Dynpro Grid to Multidimensional Analysis represents one of the most prominent analytics modernization initiatives available for your organization in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. The new MDA experience is faster, more flexible, and fully aligned with the SAP Fiori design language. But the transition only feels seamless to your business users if their saved work comes with them.

Key takeaways before the migration:

  • Identify whether your Web Dynpro Grid apps are SAP-delivered or custom — this determines whether you need 1 step or 3.
  • For custom apps, complete Steps 1 and 2 first (query migration + MDA app creation) before running F7001.
  • Run the migration early in Development or Quality to validate results.
  • Communicate the known limitations (chart settings, suppress zeros, default variants) to business users before go-live.
  • Never delete the Web Dynpro Grid app before confirming the migration is complete.

10. Official SAP Help Portal Documentation

  • Migrate Variants and Bookmarks
  • How Migration Works: Overview
  • FAQ: Migrate Variants and Bookmarks

entry.title: Digitalisierung und Skalierung von Change Management: Erkenntnisse aus der SAP Kaffee-Ecke
entry.content:
Die monatliche SAP Change und Training Kaffee-Ecke widmete sich einem hochaktuellen Thema: Wie lassen sich Change Management-Prozesse in SAP-Projekten digitalisieren und skalieren? PD Dr. Oliver Kohnke, Global Service Owner for Organizational Change Management bei SAP, gab Einblicke in die Herausforderungen, Lösungsansätze und die neue AI-Assisted User Learning and Change Management App.

Warum wird Skalierung immer wichtiger?

Die Eingangsfrage an die Teilnehmenden machte deutlich: In den meisten Organisationen ist die Digitalisierung von Change Management bereits ein wichtiges Thema. Die Hälfte der Teilnehmenden stimmten dieser Aussage voll und ganz zu.

Die zweite Frage fokussierte sich auf die Herausforderungen, die bei der Digitalisierung von Change Management bestehen und gab zentrale Hinweise, die es in der Praxis zu berücksichtigen gilt: Akzeptanz steht mit deutlichem Abstand an erster Stelle, gefolgt von Kompetenz und Widerständen. Weitere genannte Aspekte waren fehlendes Verständnis, Budget- und Kapazitätsmangel, fehlende Plattformen sowie Ängste und Verunsicherung. Der Hinweis auf "Human in the Loop" verdeutlicht: Trotz KI-Unterstützung müssen Menschen im Prozess verbleiben.

Welche Herausforderungen bestehen bei der Digitalisierung von Change Management?

Die abschließende Frage ergab, dass die praktische Erfahrung mit Change Management-Apps in der Community noch begrenzt ist – die meisten Teilnehmenden haben bisher keine Berührungspunkte mit solchen Tools gesammelt.

Was treibt die Notwendigkeit zur Digitalisierung und Skalierung von Change Management? Oliver Kohnke stellte gleich zu Beginn klar: Der technologische Wandel vollzieht sich schneller als Mitarbeitende und Organisationen ihn adaptieren können. Die zentrale Herausforderung ist dabei nicht die Technologie selbst, sondern die Adoption – also die Annahme und Nutzung neuer Technologien. Gleichzeitig ist die kontinuierliche, manuelle Anpassung von Trainings- und Change Management-Inhalten zu langsam und die Kosten zu hoch. Um diese Herausforderungen zu bewältigen, müssen Change Management und Training ebenfalls schneller und effizienter werden.

Welche Vision verfolgt SAP?

SAP möchte Organizational Change Management Services und Tools anbieten, die “fully digitized, low touch, easy to use und highly scalable” sind. Diese Skalierbarkeit ist entscheidend, da SAP weltweit eine große Anzahl von Cloud-Implementierungen begleitet. Das Ziel: Kunden und Partner sollen die Angebote einfach und schnell nutzen können.

Um skalieren zu können, ist die Digitalisierung der standardisierten Methodik und Inhalte eine wesentliche Voraussetzung.

SAP nutzt seit Jahren eine “Best Practice” Change Management Methodologie. Der SAP Change Management-Ansatz ist auf SAP’s Activate Implementierungsmethodik abgestimmt und beinhaltet sechs Dimensionen: Change Strategy, Change Leadership, Change Communication, Change Realization, Change Enablement und Change Activeness.

 

Welche digitalen Assets stellt SAP bereit?

Methodik im SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer

Die Change Management Methodik ist über den SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer kostenfrei verfügbar. Diese Kundenplattform bietet Roadmaps für verschiedene SAP-Produkte auf Basis der globalen Implementierungsmethodik.

Der Change Management-Ansatz ist dort entsprechend der Activate-Phasen mit 18 Deliverables, 72 Tasks, 50 Tools & Templates und 10 Weblinks hinterlegt. Eine Registrierung auf sap.com ist notwendig, um die Tools und Templates nutzen zu können.

Ein besonderer Vorteil: Der Roadmap Viewer hat eine direkte Verbindung zu SAP Cloud Application Lifecycle Management (CALM). Wer CALM nutzt, kann die Deliverables und Templates direkt in das Tool laden und als Change Manager damit arbeiten.

SAP Learning Journeys und Zertifizierung

Um Kunden und Partnern die Nutzung der Change Managememt Methodik zu erleichtern, bietet SAP verschiedene Learning Courses an, die in einer Learning Journey unter dem Titel "Managing the People Side of SAP Cloud Projects" zusammengefasst sind. Die Lerninhalte werden auf Basis von verschiedenen Projektszenarien praxisnah vermittelt und sind über die Plattform learning.sap.com kostenfrei verfügbar.

Abschliessen kann man die Lernpfade mit einer offiziellen SAP Zertifizierung. Die Zertifizierung erfolgt AI unterstützt: In einem interaktiven Szenario mit einem Avatar werden Prüflinge in realitätsnahen Szenarien getestet . Die Zertifizierung ist entweder über ein Abonnement des SAP Learning Hub abrufbar oder kann im Trainingsshop der SAP erworben werden.

OCM in den SAP Success Plans

Das neue Service und Support Offering der SAP, die Success Plans, beinhaltet drei aufeinander aufbauende Layers: Foundational, Advanced und Max. Der Foundational und Advanced Success Plan beinhalten bereits verschiedene Change Management und Learning Services. Für den Max Success Plan sind die Inhalte zum Zeitpunkt der Coffee Corner Session noch in Arbeit).

Alle oben beschriebenen Angebote (SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer, CALM, Learning Journeys) sind auch Teil des Foundational Success Plan, den jeder Cloud Kunde automatisch erhält.

Mit dem Advanced Success Plan bekommen Kunden darüber hinaus Zugang zu verschiedenen standardisierten Change Management- und Learning-Services und Experten. Die Angebote stehen seit Mai 2026 zur Verfügung:

Darüber hinaus sind mehrere Change Management und Learning Advisory Services für den Max Success Plan in Arbeit.

 

Was leistet die neue AI-Assisted User Learning and Change Management App?

Im Juni 2026 ging die AI-Assisted User Learning and Change Management App in die General Availability. Die Anwendung ist über den SAP Micro-App Hub verfügbar. Fokus der ersten Version sind die Lernfunktionalitäten wie AI assisted Lernbedarfsanalyse und Lerninhalterstellung. Darüber hinaus bietet die App auch erste Funktionen zur Unterstützung von Change cManagement in Projekten zur Verfügung, auf die wir uns in dieser Session konzentriert haben.

Welche Funktionen bietet die App User Learning and Change Management?

Die erste Version konzentriert sich auf vier zentrale Change-Aktivitäten:

Stakeholder-Identifikation - Der Startpunkt jedes Change-Projekts:

Auf Basis von SAP Best Practices bietet die App vordefinierte Listen mit typischen betroffenen Rollen, die kundenspezifisch anpassbar sind. Wichtige Informationen wie Anzahl der betroffenen Personen, Line of Business und regionale Verteilung lassen sich für jede Stakeholdergruppe erfassen.

Die App unterstützt derzeit SAP Best Practices für S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition und Public Edition sowie einige SuccessFactors-Module. Die Integration weiterer SAP-Lösungen ist in Arbeit.

Change Story - Das Fundament der Change-Kommunikation:

Die App strukturiert diese nach Need for Change (Current Reality, Drivers, Alternatives), Future Vision, Implementation Approach (Key Facts and Figures), Value and Impact sowie Core Messaging. Alle Informationen, die typischerweise in Entscheidungsgremien vor Projektstart erstellt und diskutiert werden, lassen sich zentral dokumentieren. Das Ziel: Keine gemischten Botschaften, sondern konsistente Kommunikation. Die Change Story kann generell und für spezifische Stakeholdergruppen erstellt werden.

Kommunikationsanalyse:

Change Manager müssen wissen, welche Kanäle verfügbar sind – von Intranet-Portalen über Employee Meetings bis zu E-Mails oder Blogs. Die App hilft bei der Identifikation und Zuordnung geeigneter Kanäle für verschiedene Stakeholder-Gruppen. Auch Aspekte wie bidirektionale vs. unidirektionale Kommunikation, Frequenz und Kosten des Kanalbespielens können so leicht berücksichtigt werden. Darüber hinaus können auch für die identifizierten Stakeholdergruppen konkrete Kommunikationsbedarfe erfasst werden.

Kommunikationsplan - Das zentrale Arbeitstool für Change Manager:

Hier werden alle Kommunikationsmaßnahmen mit Zielgruppen, Kanälen, Timing und Status geplant und können auf einer Zeitschiene visualisiert werden. Durch eine farbliche Markierung der Kommunikationsmaßnahmen wird sofort ersichtlich, ob es sich um bidirektionale Kommunikation (wie Mitarbeitermeetings) oder unidirektionale Kommunikation (wie Projektankündigungen) handelt und letztlich ein guter Kommunikationsmix geplant wurde.

Ist die App auch für Nicht-SAP-Projekte nutzbar?

Eine wichtige Frage aus der Community: Kann die App auch für andere Change-Projekte genutzt werden, etwa die Einführung eines CRM- oder TMS-Systems? Die Antwort ist eindeutig Ja. Stakeholder-Identifikation, Change Story Kommunikationskanäle – und bedarfe sowie der Kommunikationsplan können für jedes beliebige Change-Projekt genutzt werden. Der besondere Benefit für SAP-Projekte liegt in der Verknüpfung zu den SAP Business Best Practices.

Welche KI-Features sind geplant?

Bis Ende 2026 will SAP entscheiden, wo KI im Change Management-Bereich den maximalen Mehrwert bringt. Das langfristige Ziel ist ambitioniert: personalisierte Massenkommunikation für SAP-Projekte. Bei vielen betroffenen Mitarbeitenden soll die KI rollenspezifische Botschaften generieren, die nah an deren Business-Realität sind.

Wie soll das funktionieren?

Kundenspezifische Projektdokumente – PowerPoint-Präsentationen, Entscheidungsmemos, Scope-Dokumente – sollen in die App hochgeladen werden können. Darüber hinaus sollen Schnittstellen zu Signavio und CALM SAP-spezifischen Inhalt bereitstellen. Die KI extrahiert daraus die relevanten Change-Elemente für die Change Story: Was ist im Scope, warum wird das Projekt gemacht, was ist die Urgency, welche Vision wird verfolgt? Change Manager können dann darauf aufbauen und die Story mit Key Stakeholdern reflektieren und anpassen.

Weitere geplante Features sind die Ergänzung um Stakeholder-Analyse und Change Impact-Analyse – beides Kernaktivitäten eines Change Managers – sowie die automatische Generierung von rollenbasierten Kommunikationsplänen und -inhalten.

Wie wird Qualität sichergestellt?

Ein kritischer Aspekt wurde diskutiert: Die Change Story ist das Fundament der gesamten Change-Kommunikation und entsteht idealerweise im Dialog. Ist eine Tool-basierte Erstellung nicht problematisch?

Oliver Kohnke betonte: "Der Output muss reviewed werden. Es bleibt jedem Change Manager freigestellt, die Change Story mit wichtigen Stakeholdern, dem Project Sponsor oder im Projektteam zu reflektieren und anzupassen." Das Konzept des **"Human in the Loop"** ist zentral – die KI beschleunigt, ersetzt aber nicht die fachliche Bewertung.

Wie ist die Mehrsprachigkeit geregelt?

Bei internationalen Projekten ist Kommunikation in lokalen Sprachen entscheidend. Ist Mehrsprachigkeit verfügbar? Das Tool selbst ist momentan nur in Englisch verfügbar – dem Servicemodell geschuldet, um globale Skalierbarkeit zu ermöglichen.

Was kostet die App?

Die App ist über den SAP Micro-App Hub verfügbar. Die Foundational Features (Basisfunktionalitäten ohne AI) kosten 1.140 Euro pro Jahr für 5 Nutzer.

Die Lizenzen richten sich an Change Manager und Learning Manager – nicht an Endanwendende.

Wie funktioniert das Pricing für KI-Features?

Für AI-gestützte Funktionen gilt ein verbrauchsbasiertes Modell mit 7 Euro pro AI Unit**. Jede durch die KI generierte Seite Learning Content wird als "Page" gezählt. Bei der Generierung von 10.000 Pages innerhalb eines Monats würde der Monatspreis bei etwa 8.050 Euro liegen.

Welche Effizienzgewinne wurden bereits erreicht?

Die entscheidende Frage: Gibt es bereits Erfahrungen zu Produktivitätsgewinnen? Die Antwort ist beeindruckend: Ergebnisse aus dem Early Adoption Programm zeigten Effizienzgewinne von 50 bis 60 Prozent bei der AI-basierten Durchführung von Lernbedarfsanalysen und Generierung von Learninginhalten.

Fazit: Change Management wird messbar effizienter

Die Digitalisierung und Skalierung von Change Management ist keine Zukunftsvision mehr, sondern bereits Realität. SAP zeigt mit der neuen App einen pragmatischen Weg auf, wie Change Manager durch Standardisierung, Digitalisierung und KI-Unterstützung erhebliche Zeitgewinne erzielen können.

Die bereits erreichten Effizienzgewinne im Learning-Bereich zeigen auch das Potenzial für den Change Management-Bereich. Gleichzeitig bleibt die Herausforderung bestehen, Akzeptanz für diese digitalen Werkzeuge zu schaffen – genau jener Punkt, der auch in der Eingangsumfrage als größtes Hindernis identifiziert wurde.

Die Kombination aus bewährter Methodik, strukturierten Tools und intelligenter Automatisierung verspricht, Change Management in SAP-Projekten auf ein neues Effizienzniveau zu heben – ohne die notwendige menschliche Expertise und den Dialog mit Stakeholdern zu ersetzen.

Die nächste Kaffee-Ecke nach der Sommerpause widmet sich KI-Tools und Use Cases in der täglichen Arbeit – mit interaktivem Austausch in Kleingruppen statt großer Vorträge. 

Weiterführende Informationen:

  • Infos zu AI assisted User Learning and Change Management im SAP Micro-App Hub
  • Startseite SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer
  • OCM im SAP Activate Roadmap Viewer 
  • Alle SAP Lerninhalte auf learning.sap.com
  • OCM and Learning / Managing the People Side of SAP Cloud Projects
  • Kaffee-Ecke Event-Seite
  • Kontakt für Deep Dive-Sessions: oliver.kohnke@sap.com

entry.title: Re: Search help (F4) for the Person Responsible field is...

entry.title: Re: SAP Document AI Basic UI Subscription Fails in BTP Tr...
entry.content:
I have the same issue, but could narrow the problem down.

With creating the Application of Document AI and assigning policies to a user, the trust configuration between btp and the trial 
Identity Service is broken. When you go to Trust Configuration in your btp trial account and click on the linked Identity Service, the refresh of the trust does not work and returns an error message:

We're sorry, but we've encountered an internal error. If the problem persists, please create a support ticket.(Correlation ID: 94af6d88-13d1-4d49-7ec4-c96b8ada47e0)

Only the following fields are allowed in 'additionalAttributes': [domain].

This results in the issue that the document ai application does not get the correct policy assignments from the Identity Service. When the /workspace url is open for the Document AI service, the user information show the correct linkage to the Identity Service, but show empty roles. So even with an assigned Admin Policy it does rejects any changes to the user and shows no permissions.

Conclusion: Identity federation is fine, group->role/authorization federation via the OIDC trus is broken server-side on the trial tenant and the trust config can't be refreshed or (cleanly) rebuild.

entry.title: Re: DOCUMENT_MAIN03 – Update QM Inspection Lot Status from DIR Classification (CV01N)
entry.content:
If you have a API or BG job triggering this DIR creation
BAPI_DOCUMENT_CREATE2
or
BAPI_DOCUMENT_CHANGE2.

Then, directly use the STATUS_CHANGE_INTERN/EXTERN FM to update the status if sy-subrc = 0, followed by COMMIT_WORK.
As FM 'CLFM_GET_INTERNAL_TABLES' dont hold data during BAPI call.

entry.title: SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 1 in review
entry.content:
Having AI is not the same as using AI. Buying a solution is not the same as adopting it. That gap between what's deployed and what's actually running live, generating value day in and day out is exactly what SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week is built around.

Day 1 "Understand: The Enterprise AI Shift - From Vision to Action" set the foundation. Sessions covered the state of enterprise AI adoption, practical tools for building AI literacy at scale, a structured method for identifying high-value agentic AI use cases, and a candid look at what separates organizations generating real value from those still stuck in exploration mode. The day ended with a first look at Joule Work, SAP's new unified AI workspace.

Here's my recap of the day's sessions and the questions that came up most.

The opening keynote centered on SAP's vision for the Autonomous Enterprise: humans keep the decisions that require judgment, creativity, and accountability, while AI handles the coordination and execution. The strategy is set. The technology is ready. The gap is execution because AI adoption is hard work, and learning is what bridges it. From building AI literacy to hands-on product understanding, it is what enables individuals and teams to turn innovation into measurable business value.

What does it actually take to move an entire organization from AI-curious to AI-capable? It starts with AI literacy, the skills and learning that turn AI investment into real capability. With 86% of companies unable to build it fast enough, the pressure has never been higher. SAP's internal campaign, "Let's work the AI way," put the scale in perspective: 230+ AI use cases live, 500+ data products, and a value realization ambition exceeding €2Bn. The lesson from SAP's own experience: don't wait, and don't treat learning as a one-time event.

Your most asked questions:

  • What is NotebookLM and how do I access it?
    NotebookLM is an AI learning companion built into learning.sap.com. It lets you interact with course content conversationally rather than consuming it linearly, no separate activation needed, just a Learning Hub subscription. Partners report it cuts 20+ hour learning journeys down to around 5 hours.
  • What AI certifications are available and where do I start?
    Your starting point depends on your role and learning goals. Explore SAP’s AI learning portfolio on learning.sap.com/artificial-intelligence to find learning opportunities for different audiences. Business users can start with Introducing Joule and Getting Started with Joule for Business Users, while developers can work toward the SAP Certified – SAP Generative AI Developer certification. Partners can also explore role-based learning paths and Certification Weeks through the SAP Partner Portal.
  • How much time should I invest, and does format matter?
    Two hours per week is a reasonable floor for foundational AI literacy. But format matters more than hours: 2–3 day on-site bootcamps and hackathons consistently outperform self-paced resources alone, based on SAP's own adoption data.

Want to learn more about building AI literacy at scale and what SAP has learned from its own adoption journey? Rewatch Opening Keynote: SAP AI Adoption and Learning Week and AI Literacy to Impact: Skills and Change Management, and How SAP Runs SAP.

Once the literacy conversation is underway, teams hit the next wall: which problems are actually worth solving with agentic AI and how do you identify them? Not every process needs an agent. The SAP AppHaus team introduced a practical, human-centered method for making that call: the Joule Agent Discovery Workshop,  a practical, human-centered method that helps a cross-functional team identify and prioritize a high-value agentic AI use case.  A follow-on Joule Agent Design Workshop turns that into a blueprint ready for configuration in Joule Studio. All workshop materials are freely available at apphaus.sap.com. Rewatch Discovering High-Value Opportunities for Agentic AI to see them in action.

Your most asked questions:

  • Should you use agents for deterministic use cases like finance or planning?
    Yes, but constrain how the LLM handles factual data. For precise answers, give the agent a tool (an API, a database call) and require it to return that result as-is. Use the LLM only for language, summarizing or formatting the output, not generating the answer. Grounding works when the agent is explicitly prevented from going beyond the retrieved data.
  • Is there a framework for rethinking processes for Agentic AI?
    The key shift is to stop designing every step and define the outcome instead: goal, constraints, and Agent reasoning. Agents find their own path. SAP’s integration of SAP Signavio with Agent Hub goes further by using process mining to observe how Agents behave and improve from there.

Frameworks point the way, but they don't tell you what it feels like to move an entire organization pilot mode to generating real AI value. A candid fireside chat with draw on direct experience with early adopters, three themes stood out: Focus beats breadth, AI adoption is also a team sport, and none of it scales without an executive sponsor.

And the bigger ambition? The organizations that lead won't just automate what exists. They'll reinvent it. Deep dive into the key themes and Rewatch the Fireside Chat: From Exploration to Enterprise Value - Lessons from Early SAP Business AI Adopters.

So what does reinventing work actually look like in practice?  In Joule Work, users express in natural language what they want to accomplish and a dynamic workspace that, based on people's intent, surfaces the right insights and automates routine work. Once a user starts a conversation, a Joule Assistant steps in: role-based AI that understands your context and responsibilities. Assistants coordinate teams of Joule Agents, which autonomously execute complex workflows across systems and business domains. The actions agents take are defined by Joule Skills, predefined capabilities like fetching data or triggering a workflow. Early Adopter Care is planned for July 16. General availability is planned for October 2026. Rewatch Meet Joule Work.

Your most asked questions:

  • What is Joule Work, and how is it different from other Joule products?
    Joule is embedded directly into SAP solutions like S/4HANA and SuccessFactors, contextual to whatever you are doing inside that system. Joule Work sits above that: it spans your entire SAP landscape and lets you act on end-to-end processes in one place, without switching applications. The two are connected and you can launch Joule Work directly from the embedded experience in full-screen mode.
  • Are Spaces permanent? Can I share them with colleagues?
    A space is a dynamic, AI-generated workspace for simple and complex problem-solving. Spaces are persistent. They are never automatically deleted, unless you want to, and can be reused across sessions. You can pin them for quick access, and they stay exactly as you left them. On sharing: collaboration is not yet available in the current Early Adopter phase.

Day 1 set the foundation. The rest of the week is about putting it all into practice. Continue with the full series:

  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 2 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 3 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 4 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 1 in review

entry.title: The Board Decides, the CIO Delivers: A Digital Health Platform Powered by SAP
entry.content:
In my previous posts I described SAP’s global healthcare industry strategy, a modular, data-driven, open, and interoperable platform, and I argued that a digital health platform (DHP) is ultimately a business decision that belongs on the CEO’s desk. Once the board has made that call, however, the mandate lies with the CIO, who turns a strategic ambition into a governed and sequenced delivery program that runs alongside daily operations without disrupting them. This post is written for that very role, and it tries to focus on two questions: what does a digital health platform powered by SAP actually consist of, and why does it make the CIO’s task easier instead of adding yet another program to an already full portfolio?

A landscape grown by change, not by design

In my experience, most healthcare providers definitely do not suffer from a lack of applications; they suffer from a system landscape that has grown over many years and rarely by design, and the figures show this: a symplr survey of health system clinicians and CIOs found that a majority of organizations run somewhere between 50 and 500, sometimes even more, different software solutions to operate their healthcare business, and in the United States alone the sector saw more than 900 hospital merger and acquisition transactions between 2014 and 2024, each of which brought their own systems, teams, and technical debt into somebody else’s landscape. So, there is an electronic medical record (EMR) system, or several of them after mergers, one or more revenue cycle management (RCM) systems for billing and patient accounting, a sometimes aging enterprise resource planning (ERP) system, a number of departmental specialty systems, research databases, and a long tail of point solutions, each of which brings its own data model, its own integration approach, and its own vendor release schedule. In such a landscape, every new capability becomes its own project, and every project has to solve integration, data quality, and compliance all over again, and the pace of change of the whole institution is effectively set by whichever vendor moves slowest.

The timing pressure is real, too. Many hospitals still run SAP Business Suite 7 (ECC), whose mainstream maintenance ends in 2027, with extended maintenance through 2030 and a Transition Option in SAP Private Cloud through 2033, and in Europe the secondary-use provisions of the European Health Data Space (EHDS) Regulation begin to apply in March 2029, which means the CIO’s delivery window for both obligations is essentially the same three to four years. And the platform market itself is being shaped in exactly that window: Gartner estimated in its April 2024 research "Innovation Insight: Digital Health Platforms Accelerate Transformation" that the digital health platform market was only around five percent penetrated, with adoption expected to play out over five to ten years, so the institutions making their architectural choices now are the ones whose requirements the market will form around.

The EHR-first instinct, and where it ends

Before laying out our answer, let me name the objection I hear most often, because it deserves a serious analysis. Many health systems reasonably prefer to look at their EMR vendor first for any new capability; successive Bain and KLAS surveys have found that roughly two out of three look first to their existing vendors, and especially their EMR vendor, before considering anyone else. I understand the instinct, since the EMR carries the clinical workflows and much of the clinical data, and nobody wants another vendor relationship without good reason. Yet the same market research paints a sobering picture of how that instinct works out in practice, with only about a quarter of executives in a Sage Growth survey saying their EMR vendor fully lives up to its promises. The deeper issue, though, is not vendor performance but scope: the decisions a CIO is now asked to support, from cost transparency per procedure to workforce planning to supply chain resilience to research data provisioning, depend on finance, procurement, workforce, and logistics data that does not live in the EMR and never will, and a system built to document and bill for care was simply never designed to be the enterprise data and AI foundation of a health system. This is, incidentally, also where the analyst community has landed: Gartner has addressed its digital health platform research explicitly to healthcare provider CIOs since its initial Market Guide in 2021, describing a shift away from monolithic, EMR-centric application suites towards a platform architecture, and in its most recent framing "Digital Health Platforms (Transitioning to Healthcare Provider Industry Cloud Platforms) Reviews and Ratings" positions the category as evolving into Healthcare Provider Industry Cloud Platforms whose purpose is to let a CIO adjust the application portfolio at the pace of business change. That last phrase describes the job better than I ever could.

Connect, govern, innovate, transform

Our strategy for the digital health platform powered by SAP addresses exactly this in four dimensions: connect, govern, innovate, and transform. Let me take them in the order an actual delivery program would.

To connect means to bring disparate systems into a single, coherent view across clinical and enterprise operations. The platform speaks the standards your landscape already uses, among them HL7 FHIR®, openEHR, IHE (Integrating the Healthcare Enterprise), OMOP (the common data model of the Observational Medical Outcomes Partnership), and, in Germany, ISiK as the national interoperability standard for hospital information systems. It connects EMR and revenue cycle management systems as well as EHDS-related sources, alongside SAP and non-SAP applications, whether they run as software as a service, cloud-native, hybrid, or on-premises. The AnyEMR/MultiEMR and AnyRCM/MultiRCM principles I described in the strategy post apply here, too: the platform integrates across whatever clinical and billing systems you have chosen rather than locking you into a single vendor’s stack, and since SAP does not build EMR or RCM systems itself, we unify across them together with and through our partner ecosystem.

To govern is, for the CIO, the most consequential of the four: in most institutions today, governance is a per-project exercise: every integration project defines its own access rules, every research request triggers its own anonymization effort, every audit finding produces its own remediation plan, and the sum of all this is a patchwork of controls that nobody can fully describe, let alone defend under time pressure. On the platform, governance becomes a managed capability instead. Master data governance, identity and access, consent and rights management, pseudonymization and anonymization, and data sovereignty compliance are built into the architecture and travel with every data product, so a rule defined once, for example who may see identifiable patient data and under which consent, is enforced wherever that data flows, whether into an operational dashboard, an AI model, or a research extract. That does not make audit readiness automatic, because audits will always also examine processes and people, but it makes audit readiness sustainable, since the technical controls no longer have to be reconstructed and re-documented for every new use of the data. And where residency requirements demand it, the same architecture can be deployed on sovereign-cloud options, so that the jurisdiction question is answered by design rather than negotiated per project. This is also where regulation stops being only a burden: an institution whose consent, provenance, and de-identification are managed capabilities enters the EHDS era prepared, while one that handles them project by project will spend those same years catching up.

To innovate then builds on that foundation rather than around it: a composition layer with analytics from descriptive to predictive, knowledge graphs for reasoning across clinical, operational, and financial domains, healthcare-specific semantics and ontologies, which are expanding to include terminologies such as SNOMED CT and the OMOP data models, and AI domain models including clinical natural language processing, all accessible through build tools from low-code to pro-code, so that extensions live safely outside a clean core and survive the next upgrade, a lesson many of us learned the hard way in the ERP world. And to transform is simply what follows when the first three are in place: decisions powered by governed data and embedded AI, at a pace the institution sets for itself.

The architecture, seen from the delivery side

At the top level, the DHP architecture consists of three layers, and increasingly a fourth. A data foundation provides clinical, enterprise, and ecosystem connectivity, including the healthcare interoperability standards mentioned above. A digital innovation layer carries the intelligence and the health-specific business context. A healthcare experiences layer offers applications such as patient portals, clinical workplaces, health apps and dashboards, and scenarios like clinical trial matching, patient identity resolution, and the EHDS health record. And on top of these sits the AI layer, in which embedded intelligence, assistants, and increasingly agentic capabilities operate on the governed layers beneath them rather than beside them. Readers who know Gartner’s published DHP reference architecture from their "Market Guide for Digital Health Platforms" will recognize the shape of the first three: an integrated data fabric with health data connectors, data curation and enrichment, metadata management, a healthcare-specific data model, and privacy and consent controls; a layer of packaged business capabilities; and a composition layer with low-code tooling for building tailored experiences. Our architecture is a concrete instantiation of exactly that pattern, and it deliberately extends it in the one dimension that has moved fastest since the pattern was first described: the AI layer on top. And for readers who want to explore how this platform category is developing across the wider market, Vince Kuraitis’s series “Platforming Healthcare – The Long View” offers a thorough long-term perspective.

What binds these layers together is the data product, and it comes in two kinds. Primary data products are sourced directly from a clinical or enterprise application and represent the original, unprocessed data: claims records from the revenue cycle management system, encounter summaries from the EMR, patient records from a clinical data repository (CDR), or financials from SAP Cloud ERP. Derived data products are curated and enriched on top of them, and here, health-specific content such as Patient 360, treatment cost, clinical workforce readiness, and patient risk scores, which deliver ready-to-use insight without additional integration effort, are being built out. Anyone who has run a hospital integration program knows why this matters: instead of maintaining n×m point-to-point integrations between sources and consumers, you build governed, reusable assets once and consume them everywhere, with lineage and policy enforced from the start.

This is what that AI layer changes in practice: because it is grounded in exactly this governed, contextualized data, and because it acts through the platform’s managed capabilities rather than around them, AI can be deployed where it demonstrably relieves pressure in daily operations. SAP Joule, our AI assistant, is being extended by SAP and partners with healthcare scenarios to address the everyday pain points of running a hospital, from clinical documentation, coding and DRG optimization, and denial management to medication replenishment, patient scheduling, regulatory reporting, and workforce planning, while differentiating industry scenarios around Patient 360, clinical workforce optimization, and denial prevention and revenue recovery build on the very same foundation. The CIO needs neither a separate AI stack nor a separate governance regime for it, and no separate integration program to feed it, because the platform itself is the AI foundation. It is also the path to what we at SAP call the Autonomous Enterprise: operations in which routine work increasingly runs itself, under the institution's own rules and controls. That kind of autonomy cannot be installed; it becomes possible only once connected data, business context, and governance are already in place, which is exactly what the layers beneath the AI provide.

That this approach works in practice is not a hypothesis: Hirslanden, Switzerland’s largest private hospital group, modernized its core processes and systems to support new clinical practices, regulatory standards, and cost reimbursement using SAP Signavio business transformation solutions, an example of how process intelligence grounded in a common platform accelerates transformation in healthcare rather than adding to the project load.

An ecosystem by design

No single vendor covers healthcare end to end, and we at SAP do not pretend to. Partners extend the platform with the clinical capabilities and industry solutions, built on SAP Business Technology Platform and SAP Business Data Cloud, delivered through SAP PartnerEdge, and available via SAP Store. For the CIO, that translates into freedom of choice at the edges and a stable, standards-based core in the middle.

A pragmatic sequence, not a big bang

Given the maintenance and regulatory horizons described above, a deliberate sequence is in fact the lower-risk path. It begins with mapping the landscape and agreeing on three to five cross-functional use cases that span clinical and operational domains. It continues with setting up the data foundation and publishing the first data products with governance in place, and with modernizing the ERP core at the institution’s own pace, be it with GROW with SAP for first-time adopters or with RISE with SAP for existing landscapes, where most providers favor separating the ERP and hospital information system transformations, with ERP first, to harmonize master data before clinical systems are subsequently integrated. It then connects the EMR, RCM, and priority partner solutions through standards-based integration including HL7 FHIR®, and it proves value early with time-boxed pilots before scaling responsibly.

The outcome is what the board asked for in the first place, and it is what running best means in healthcare: an institution that adopts innovation at its own speed, meets its regulatory obligations by design, and, where it chooses to, takes part in emerging health data collaborations on its own terms. I will not promise any CIO that a platform shrinks the portfolio overnight, because none does, and every practitioner knows that platforms are additive in the first place before they are consolidating. What it does provide is something more important: an operational model with a credible, governed path from a landscape that dictates its own terms to one that follows yours.

entry.title: Re: ECP to Banking Integration - What are the best options?
entry.content:
SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity (MBC) is a cloud-based, multi-bank digital hub that simplifies and automates the financial connection between your SAP ERP system (such as SAP S/4HANA or SAP ERP Central Component) and your financial institutions (banks).

It is a fully managed Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) solution hosted on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).

Key Capabilities and Features
  • Automated Payment Processing: It automatically sends payment instructions created in your SAP system directly to your banks.
  • Automated Bank Statements: It retrieves electronic bank statements, lockbox files, and payment status notifications from your banks and routes them back into your SAP system for automated reconciliation.
  • Direct and Network Connectivity:
    • Direct Host-to-Host (H2H): Establishes secure, direct connections with your core banks.
    • SWIFT Integration: Offers embedded access to the SWIFT network, allowing you to reach thousands of banks globally through a single channel.
  • High Security: It secures corporate-to-bank communication using strong encryption, digital signatures, and secure protocols (such as AS2 or SFTP), ensuring end-to-end data integrity.
  • Format Conversion: It handles the translation of payment formats (such as converting SAP payment data into ISO 20022 XML, MT101, or country-specific formats) required by different banks.

More information here: - SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity | SAP Help Portal | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 2 in review
entry.content:
In my Day 1 recap, I talked about the gap between AI investment and AI value, and closed with the ambition that the organizations that lead won't just automate what exists, they will reinvent it. Day 2 was where that ambition got concrete, showing what reinvention looks like in Finance, Customer Experience, and Supply Chain, and giving consultants a practical session on getting SAP Joule for Consultants running on live projects.

Here's my recap of the day's sessions and the questions that came up most.

AI is everywhere. Results aren't.

That was the opening line of the Day 2 Morning Briefing (recording), and it set the tone for everything that followed. 74% of organizations are stuck in pilot and the reason is structural: generic AI lacks business context, point solutions create patchwork, and building without governance slows everything down. Most enterprises are still assembling AI function by function, a copilot here, a point solution there. Each adds intelligence to a silo. None of them talk to each other.

SAP's answer is one system. The SAP Autonomous Suite is organized around five domains, Finance, Spend, Supply Chain, HR, and Customer Experience, where people direct, Assistants coordinate, and Agents execute. Domains move as one: a decision in Finance shows up in Supply Chain; a change in demand triggers a response in Procurement. Industry AI extends each domain with vertical knowledge and regulatory logic from day one. Explore all Joule Assistants here.

On Day 1, we looked at Joule Work as the unified workspace where this all comes together. Day 2 went deeper, showing what the Autonomous Enterprise means for Finance, CX, and Supply Chain specifically. The sessions that followed answered exactly that. Rewatch them:

  • From SAP Sapphire to What's Next in Finance: See how a billing process map changes when agents take over the work that shouldn't need a person (recording).
  • From SAP Sapphire to What's Next in Supply Chain Management: Get a sneak peek of the Asset and Service Assistant (recording).
  • From SAP Sapphire to What's Next in Customer Experience: See how CX becomes a revenue function by closing the disconnect between engagement data and business outcomes (recording).

Seeing what the Autonomous Enterprise looks like in each domain is the first step. Getting there is the next. The day's final session was built for the consultants and partners doing that work. Two features released at SAP Sapphire 2026 were the headline of this session, and both are already available:

Custom Knowledge Grounding connects SAP Joule for Consultants to your organization's own content such as project files, internal knowledge repositories, delivery standards, and policies indexed via SAP AI Core Grounding. The result: answers that draw on SAP's full knowledge base and your firm's institutional knowledge at the same time. Less time searching. Responses that reflect how your organization actually works.

  • SAP Joule for Consultants Custom Knowledge Grounding
  • Enhance SAP Joule for Consultants with Custom Knowledge Grounding

Expert Workspace enables persistent personalization in SAP Joule for Consultants by introducing reusable Experts with custom instructions and file-based grounding. It is a centralized environment within the SAP Joule for Consultants console where users can create, configure, and manage Experts at design time. Experts can then be explicitly activated during conversations at run time.

  • SAP Joule for Consultants Expert Workspace
  • Getting Started with SAP Joule for Consultants Expert Workspace

Rewatch From Activation to Application: Getting Started with SAP Joule for Consultants.

Before I wrap up, I wanted to address the questions that came up most:

  • What does it actually cost?
    SAP Joule for Consultants is a premium AI feature.     Pricing is user-based, reflecting scenarios where value is directly tied to individual productivity. It is offered as a Per User Per Month package, with a fixed number of 35 AI Units per user. Consumption is tracked in SAP for Me. Find out more in the Whitepaper Understanding the SAP Business AI Commercial Model.
  • What are the prerequisites for activating SAP Joule for Consultants?
    As a customer or partner, you will need to have AI Units and a SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) Global Account. There are no other technical dependencies and no system requirements. SAP Joule for Consultants is a standalone web-based assistant, which means on-premise, ECC, and private cloud customers can activate and use it just as easily as public cloud customers. Find out how to enable SAP Joule for Consultants in the Discovery Center Mission.

Day 1 set the foundation. Day 2 put it into practice. Continue with the full series:

  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 3 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 4 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 1 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 2 in review

entry.title: Withholding Tax Not Recognized in SD Customer Invoices
entry.content:
Hello all,

We need to issue customer invoices and credit notes through the SD module with withholding tax applied to customers. However, the withholding tax is not being recognized or calculated by the system during the billing process.

Business Scenario Our business requires that certain customers are subject to withholding tax, which should be reflected in the SD billing documents and the corresponding accounting postings.

Issue Although the relevant customer master data and withholding tax settings have been maintained, the system does not determine or apply withholding tax when creating SD billing documents. As a result, the withholding tax amount is not reflected in the invoice or in the related accounting document.

Could you please help us understand:

  1. Whether this scenario is supported in SAP S/4HANA Cloud.
  2. The required configuration steps to enable withholding tax for customer invoices created through SD billing.
  3. Any prerequisites, business roles, master data settings, tax codes, or SSCUIs that need to be maintained.
  4. Any known limitations regarding withholding tax processing in SD billing documents.

Thank you for your help.

Kind regards,

Marlene

entry.title: Re: Practice System for SAP BTP Admin Certification
entry.content:
Hi @Arulgsenthil 

The C_ADBTP SAP Certified - SAP BTP Administrator certification is a system-based assessment, which means it is different from a traditional multiple-choice exam. 
Instead of answering questions, you will be asked to complete practical tasks in a live SAP environment. This allows you to demonstrate your hands-on skills and show that you can perform common SAP BTP administration activities in realistic scenarios. 

To help you prepare, I recommend reviewing the onboarding materials available to candidates, especially the SAP Certification System-Based Assessment Video. The video explains the assessment experience step by step, from scheduling your assessment to completing the tasks and earning your certification badge.

You can find more information on the certification page: C_ADBTP SAP Certified - SAP BTP Administrator 

For additional details, visit our Help Center

I hope this helps, and best of luck with your certification journey!

Best regards,
Margit Wagner
