entry.title: PO Workflow Approval Clarification
entry.content:
Hello Team,

We need a clarification on the process of PO Workflow Approval through My Inbox in FIORI.

In one of my cases, we have designed 5 steps and at each step we have given the PO value as the distinct parameter to trigger that particular step.

When the PO is created, it was approved at the first 2 levels and then went to the inbox of 3rd level of approver, but before taking any action on the 3rd level the value of the PO was reduced to such a level that only first 2 level of approvers were required to approve.

Now the question is if PO is still in the inbox of 3rd level of approver but after value change only first 2 approver were sufficient to approve the PO (which they have already approved) then why PO is still stuck with the 3rd level of approver.

Why not the PO set as approved automatically and skipping all the higher level of approvers.

entry.title: Distributed Energy Resources Are Becoming the Operating Model for Modern Utilities
entry.content:
Distributed Energy Resources Are Becoming the Operating Model for Modern Utilities

A practical industry perspective on DER, grid-edge transformation, prosumer participation, AI-enabled orchestration, and why utilities need to act now.

Key message: Distributed Energy Resources are no longer peripheral assets; they are becoming core building blocks of a more flexible, decentralized, customer-centric energy system.

 

The energy system is moving from centralized supply to distributed orchestration

Utilities worldwide are entering a new era in which energy is no longer generated exclusively from centralized power plants. Millions of distributed assets—including solar panels, batteries, EV chargers, and flexible loads—are becoming active participants in grid operations. For utilities, this transition creates both operational complexity and strategic opportunity. The traditional model of one-way power flow from centralized generation to passive consumers is giving way to a dynamic ecosystem of prosumers, flexible loads, grid-edge devices, market platforms, and intelligent control systems.

The next phase of utility modernization will depend on the ability to model, monitor, forecast, integrate, and orchestrate distributed assets at scale. DER management is therefore not just a technology agenda—it is becoming a core operating model for the future utility.

 

Market signals point to accelerating DER investment

Multiple market indicators suggest that DER-related capabilities will continue to attract significant utility investment across the decade:

Market indicator

Signal

DERMS market

Projected to grow from approximately USD 0.93B in 2026 to USD 3.79B by 2034, at around 19% CAGR

Virtual Power Plant market

Projected to expand from approximately USD 7.4B in 2026 to USD 30.9B by 2033

Smart grid market

Forecast to grow from approximately USD 34.7B in 2023 to USD 114.2B by 2030

EV charging infrastructure market

Projected to grow from approximately USD 25.37B in 2023 to USD 227.24B by 2030

Battery Energy Storage System market

Projected to reach approximately USD 105.96B by 2030

Public EV charging points

Expected to exceed 15 million globally by 2030

Global energy storage capacity

Expected to increase sixfold to around 1,500 GW by 2030

What counts as a Distributed Energy Resource?

DERs are decentralized energy assets connected at the distribution network, grid-edge, or customer premises level. They support localized energy generation, storage, flexible consumption, and grid services. Thereby transforming customers from passive Energy customers into active participants in the overall energy ecosystem.

 

Typical examples of DER as follows

  • Rooftop solar photovoltaic systems
  • Residential, commercial, and utility-scale battery storage
  • Electric vehicles and EV charging infrastructure
  • Smart thermostats and connected home energy devices
  • Demand response and load flexibility programs
  • Distributed generation assets
  • Microgrids and community energy networks
  • Renewable gas and green hydrogen assets

Why DER matters for utilities

For Utilities, DERs provide a critical foundation to support modern grid operations and customer-centric energy services. Key Industry drivers for Utility DER can be slated below categories

Decarbonization: Utilities face increasing pressure to support net-zero commitments, integrate renewables, and reduce carbon intensity across the value chain.

Prosumers: Customers are becoming active participants who generate, store, consume, and sell energy back to the grid, creating new needs for visibility, settlement, engagement, and orchestration.

EV growth: Electric vehicles introduce new and variable charging loads, requiring utilities to manage demand peaks, distribution constraints, charging infrastructure, and tariff innovation at scale.

Grid modernization: Modern grids need real-time visibility, forecasting, control, and coordination across distributed assets to maintain stability and improve resilience.

Regulatory expectations: Policy and regulatory frameworks are increasingly encouraging renewable integration, demand flexibility, customer participation, and transparent program management.

The capability areas Utilities need to grow:

As DER adoption grows, Utilities will need a connected capability stack that spans grid operations, enterprise systems, customer programs, data platforms, market mechanisms, and cybersecurity.  The table below highlights the key focus areas where utility organizations should invest and strengthen capabilities to capitalize on emerging market opportunities, enable sustainable growth, and position themselves for long-term success in the evolving energy landscape

Capability area

Utility business need

DERMS

Centralized monitoring, control, and orchestration of distributed energy assets

Virtual Power Plants

Aggregation and dispatch of distributed resources to support grid flexibility and market participation

Grid flexibility platforms

Balancing supply and demand through flexible load management and resource optimization

EV charging management

Scalable management of EV demand, infrastructure, tariffs, and grid constraints

Flexibility markets

Monetization of distributed flexibility through market participation, settlement, and incentive programs

Smart meter analytics

Visibility into consumption, generation, load behavior, and grid-edge performance

AI-based forecasting

Prediction of demand, renewable generation, EV charging patterns, and network constraints

Customer energy platforms

Digital engagement for prosumers, demand response participants, and energy service customers

DER cybersecurity

Protection of distributed assets, grid-edge communications, operational data, and connected devices

Enterprise platforms and DER – seamless integration

DER programs cannot succeed as isolated grid-edge deployments. Utilities need enterprise-grade integration between customer systems, asset models, metering data, market interfaces, IoT platforms, outage systems, analytics layers, and operational technology environments.

SAP Distributed Energy Resources (SAP DER), built on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), provides a digital backbone for managing DER-related measurements, assets, customers, market relationships, and settlement processes across the utility value chain. It helps Utilities to manage multi-directional energy flows, and connect DER data into billing, settlement, customer engagement, and operational reporting processes.

The practical goal is to create a common digital foundation where DER assets, measurements, customers, programs, and grid events can be connected into a coherent operating model.

AI will move DER from monitoring to intelligent orchestration

AI-led DER capabilities can help utilities move beyond traditional system implementation toward predictive, adaptive, and increasingly autonomous operations. High-value use cases include:

  • Forecasting solar, storage, distributed generation, and flexible load behavior
  • Predicting EV charging demand by location, time period, customer segment, and network constraint area
  • Identifying congestion, voltage issues, reliability risks, and localized grid constraints before they become operational incidents
  • Automating demand response dispatch based on grid conditions, customer participation rules, and program objectives
  • Supporting dynamic load balancing across feeders, DER assets, customer programs, and flexibility resources

Insight: The most valuable DER programs will combine grid-edge telemetry, enterprise context, market signals, and AI-enabled decision support—not just standalone DERMS implementations.

A practical roadmap for utility DER transformation

Utilities should adopt a phased (0–6 Months, 6–12 Months, 12–24 Months) strategy to build, industrialize, and monetize its Utility DER offering. The recommended roadmap should focus on establishing core capabilities, creating market-ready propositions, and scaling DER-led revenue opportunities through SAP, DERMS, AI, and managed services-led engagement models. By following this phased roadmap, utilities can systematically build the capabilities required to become leaders in the decentralized energy ecosystem. The combination of SAP-powered enterprise processes, DERMS-enabled grid intelligence, AI-driven analytics, and managed services-led engagement models creates a scalable foundation for innovation, operational excellence, and long-term market growth in the evolving energy landscape.

  1. Establish the foundation: Define the DER strategy, operating model, data architecture, integration principles, regulatory requirements, and priority business outcomes.
  2. Build the digital backbone: Integrate DER asset data, measurement data, customer program data, grid systems, enterprise platforms, analytics, and cybersecurity controls.
  3. Industrialize priority use cases: Focus on DERMS integration, EV charging management, demand response, VPP enablement, smart meter analytics, forecasting, and prosumer engagement.
  4. Scale AI-led operations: Introduce forecasting, constraint prediction, exception management, automation, and managed-service operating models for continuous value realization.

Conclusion: DER is the new utility operating model

Distributed Energy Resources are no longer emerging-edge experiments. They are becoming the foundation of a more flexible, resilient, decentralized, and customer-centric energy system. The priority is not simply to add more DER assets to the network. The real challenge is to integrate, forecast, orchestrate, settle, secure, and optimize those assets as part of mainstream grid and customer operations.

Utilities that build strong DER capabilities now will be better positioned to manage renewable integration, support electric vehicle adoption, unlock demand flexibility, improve grid resilience, and create new customer value propositions. In that sense, DER is not just a technology initiative, it is the operating model of the future utility.

Together, SAP and TCS can help utilities transition from traditional centralized grid operations to a prosumer-driven, decentralized, and intelligent energy ecosystem. SAP DER provides the digital foundation and data orchestration platform, TCS delivers utility business transformation, system integration, AI-led operations, and managed services—creating an end-to-end DER transformation offering for the future utility enterprise

Data source Gratitude:  Gartner, IDC, BloombergNEF, Guidehouse Insights, Markets & Markets, Grand View Research, SAP Industry Research

entry.title: Random Day Surprise Is Back and You May Have Just Earned Our Newest Badge!
entry.content:
SAP Community is always looking for fun and meaningful ways to recognize the contributions that make this site so valuable. Today, we’re thrilled to reintroduce a fan favorite program, with a fresh twist.

Random Day Surprise is back!

Random Day Surprise is a badge program designed to celebrate the everyday moments of engagement that keep our community thriving. On select (and secret!) days throughout the year, we’ll recognize members who jump in and contribute! It could be by asking a thoughtful question, sharing expertise, writing a blog, reacting to a post, or helping another member.

You won’t know when the next surprise day will be and that’s half the fun. 

Meet the Mascot: No Drama Llama
Every great program needs a great mascot, and ours is here to keep things light, friendly, and a little bit silly: No Drama Llama

Each Random Day Surprise badge will feature No Drama Llama, paired with playful art inspired by the day itself. Expect creative, collectible designs that reflect both the spirit of the occasion and the personality of our community.

While we fondly wave goodbye to our beloved chicken mascot from the original program,

we’re excited to welcome the llama into the spotlight, inspired by the No Drama Llama who made a memorable appearance at last year’s SAP TechEd.

How It Works
There is nothing to sign up for. Just keep doing what you already do:

  • Ask questions
  • Share expertise
  • Write blogs
  • Answer others
  • React and engage

On select secret days, your contributions will unlock a surprise badge featuring No Drama Llama. Badges will appear on your profile the following day, no action is needed on your end. To check your earned badges, click the My Community Profile link from the avatar flyout menu in the header of community.sap.com.  

You’ll see your most recently earned badges in the top right corner and you can see all the badges earned from the View all badges link.

Activity counts if it happens on the recognized day, plus or minus six hours (based on UTC), giving all members of our global community a fair shot no matter what their time zone.

Surprise #1: World Emoji Day (July 17)
We kicked things off with our first recognition for the new program tied to World Emoji Day, which this year fell on on Friday, July 17.
World Emoji Day       World Emoji Day Bonus

If you engaged in SAP Community on July 17, or within the six hour window before or after, take a look at your profile, where you should find a No Drama Llama greeting you.


Whether you reacted with a like, shared insights, asked a question, or helped solve someone’s problem, your participation counted. Thank you for showing up and making the community what it is. We also awarded a special bonus badge for anyone who used an emoji in a post during the same time frame: Random Day Surprise - World Emoji Day Bonus

What’s Next?
Random Day Surprise will pop up throughout the rest of the year, sometimes tied to global moments, sometimes just because. We won’t reveal the dates in advance, but we might drop a few hints along the way… Here are a few hints of days to watch out for:

  • Big ideas and the people behind them
  • The things that help us grow
  • Small joys that keep us going
  • Quiet contributions that make a difference


Can you guess any of them? What day would you love to see celebrated in SAP Community? Drop your ideas and guesses in the comments below.

entry.title: Re: SAP Integration Suite : GitHub integration in SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Hi!

Why is only GitHub supported? Our customers use the whole range of available platforms. We use Bitbucket, but we also work with Azure DevOps and GitLab at different customers. I have created Influence Request https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/378829 for general Git support.

And why are only 50 repositories supported? In larger landscapes with one Repo per Integration Package, we will reach the limit quite soon.

And having to enter the PAT for every Git operation will drive developers mad quite quickly!

Kind regards,
Sebastian

entry.title: Memory accumulation issue in Windows Service (Crystal Reports)
entry.content:
For snowlotus:
I have read many of your insightful replies on the community.
We have a VB.NET unattended Windows Service running 24/7 on Windows Server 2016 using Crystal Reports runtime 13.0.2400.0 for label printing.
 
We strictly call ReportDocument.Close() followed by Dispose() within Finally block after every print job.
However, we observe that the Private Bytes baseline rises gradually over time. Memory partially drops during idle periods, but the minimum memory watermark cannot return to the initial level. Memory can only be fully reclaimed after restarting the service process.
We suspect this behavior comes from process-wide static caches maintained inside crpe32.dll, which cannot be cleared via public .NET APIs even after disposing report instances.
Could you help clarify two questions:
  1. Is this gradual memory baseline growth expected normal behavior of crpe32.dll?
  2. If this cache accumulation is by design, will continuous growth eventually cause OutOfMemory exceptions once memory reaches the threshold?
Thank you for your advice.

entry.title: Data Management in the Age of AI: Lessons from the Field
entry.content:
Reflections from a conversation with Chris Sam, Principal Architect at SAP and a seasoned SAP data management practitioner and consultant

Every so often, you have a conversation that crystallizes decades of industry wisdom into a few clear themes. That's what happened during a recent interview with Chris Sam, a data professional who has spent 18 years at SAP working across the full spectrum of data management. From building data warehouses in the early days, to SAP Business Objects tools like SAP Data Services and SAP Information Steward, to today's end-to-end data governance and conversion work in ERP implementations, his journey mirrors the evolution of our discipline itself.

What emerged from our conversation wasn't a list of shiny new techniques or product pitches. Instead, it was something more valuable: a grounded, honest set of best practices for organizations that want to treat data as the strategic asset it truly is. Here's what he shared, and why it matters now more than ever.

From Tactical Firefighting to Holistic Strategy

One of the first observations he made cut straight to the heart of the problem: "A lot of customers are ready to do the tactical activity — governance, cleansing, profiling — but I don't see a holistic strategy."

This is a pattern many of us in the data community recognize. Organizations jump into projects because something is broken. A migration is looming. A dashboard shows conflicting numbers. An AI initiative stalls because the data isn't trustworthy. But without a coherent end-to-end vision — one that treats data as an asset rather than a commodity — those tactical wins rarely add up to lasting transformation.

He put it simply: "Every company is a data company now. Data is foundational for everything." And with AI now dominating conversations at every customer site, that foundational role has become impossible to ignore. AI requires high-quality data. Full stop. Without a data management strategy in place, AI ambitions collapse under the weight of inconsistent, incomplete, or ungoverned information.

Data Management is a Journey, not a Switch

When asked for his top piece of advice, he offered a deceptively simple principle: data management is a journey.

"It's not like you just flip on a button and it automatically fixes everything," he said. "It takes time. It takes investment. And it takes understanding why it's important."

He acknowledged, with a laugh, that data isn't glamorous. It's not a flashy dashboard or a beautiful visualization. Most people in an organization don't understand it, which is why it gets overlooked and underestimated. But those of us who love the discipline know: without clean, well-governed data underneath, none of the pretty stuff works.

Organizational Commitment Is Non-Negotiable

The second-best practice he emphasized — and returned to throughout the conversation — was organizational commitment. This is where he sees the biggest gap in the customers he serves.

"When I go into customers and talk to the business folks and different stewards, without proper ownership, they don't take me seriously. Data has to be important to that organization."

He recounted an experience from his years at a major energy company, where the analytics platform was already delivering tremendous value, but the absence of clear data management principles meant the organization was leaving significant value on the table. Timestamps weren't being used consistently. Everyone was extracting their own customer data. If a senior leader asked five people how many customers the company had, they got five wildly different answers — not even in the same ballpark.

The fix wasn't a new tool. It was consistency, definitions, and ownership. A data dictionary, a shared understanding of what "customer" or "account" even means. And most importantly, someone accountable of the definition and data standards.

Who Needs to Be at the Table?

This led naturally to a question that plagues every data management initiative: who needs to be involved? Chris’s answer was clear: data owners and stewards — real ones, not nominal ones — and he was candid about how hard they are to find.

"Many people I encounter on the customer side are operational. They're trying to get a task done — ship materials, send invoices. They don't have that clear data owner saying, 'Making sure material data is high quality, it's correct, it's extended to the right plants — that's my responsibility.'"

The people who typically show up to data meetings, he noted, are either core business team members who understand processes but not the underlying data, or IT team members who understand the technical layer but not the business meaning. The magic happens with data stewards, people who sit in the line of business, know how the process works, know the data, and are passionate about both.

That combination is rare. And it's usually not someone's full-time job. But that role, he insisted, is critical infrastructure for any organization serious about data.

Data Products and Consistency at Scale

One of the most encouraging shifts he's seeing is the rise of data products, the idea of productizing customer, vendor, and material data so that everyone in the organization is looking at the same trusted source.

"They didn't want random people dumping tables and coming up with their own version of the truth," he said, describing what customers were trying to solve nearly a decade ago. Today, with platforms like SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) and SAP Master Data Governance (MDG), that vision is more achievable than ever.

He also highlighted an often-overlooked truth: data quality must be enforced at the point of creation. Whether through user entry validation or API-level checks during integration, catching bad data at the source is far more effective than cleaning it up downstream after it has spread across systems.

Closing Thoughts

Perhaps the most striking part of our conversation was the sense of vindication — and mild exasperation — that comes from watching an industry finally catch up to what data professionals have been saying for 20 years: Data quality matters. Governance matters. Ownership matters. AI hasn't changed that; it has only made it urgent.

The path forward isn't complicated, but it isn't easy either. Start with strategy. Commit organizationally. Find your data owners. Define your terms. Treat data as a product. And remember, it's a journey.

The rest of us data geeks will keep cheering you on.

 

Interested in learning more about Data Governance?  Check out:

  • From Silos to Strategy: What 20 Years of SAP Data Management Taught One Practitioner
  • The Challenges You Face and How SAP Business Data Cloud Helps (focused on Data Stewards and the topic of Data Governance)
  • The Day in the Life of a Data Steward – Meet Susan
  • Why master data management is the foundation of AI and digital transformation

entry.title: Re: Processing Electronic Invoices in France with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance
entry.content:
Hi @erika_buson 

Based on the discussion above, my understanding is that the communication from SAP is established with SAP DRC Cloud Edition (not directly to Peppol service in DRC CE), and the invoice is then submitted internally via the Peppol network. Is that correct?

Thank you in advance for your support.

entry.title: Re: Memory accumulation issue in Windows Service (Crystal...
entry.content:
Hi snowlotus,

Your post was rejected because you had a link to a 3rd party web site, you should have got a notice when clicking OK button...

AS you likely know Microsoft has multiple memory leaks, since CR is wrapped around MS's Dll's those leaks due accumulate.

Nothing CR can do about them, SAP actually wrote their own MemManager based on MS's code to close up as many as they could.

Tests have shown that once you hit around 100 report/iterations the memory tends to settle down.

MS is aware of the issue, IIS application often have memory issues to to leaks. MS recommends to cycle IIS once a day to clean up the fragmented memory.

I found a tool that may help you called Wise Memory Manager:

https://apps.microsoft.com/detail/xp9kl8b0zvqbxq?hl=en-GB&gl=US

It was created by the maker SysInternals, MS acquired them years ago, they have great Windows tools.

I use it because Video's from YouTube leak like crazy often exceeding 1.5 Gig, Wise Memory Manager releases it routinely resulting in memory below 1 gig now.

Not sure if it will work for you but give it a try.

The other suggestion is to clean up MS's \Temp folders, if crpe32/64 crashes it doesn't have a chance to clean up the temp files. Manually deleting them is the only way... possibly create a batch file to delete them on start up or when ever, typically there are 5 or 6 files locked by the system so skip those.

1. Yes it's normal behaviour. As I noted once the app hits 100 reports typically the memory tends to level off.

2. It's not by design but due to third party dll's that leak, mostly MS dll's and other dependencies like printer dll's.

If the above doesn't help I suggest setting your application to cycle once a day when low usage is noted...

Don

entry.title: Re: About planning calendar
entry.content:
Dear @ZZ3987,

The creation of planning calendars is possible. Although the classic Create Planning Calendar (MD25) and Change Planning Calendar (MD26) applications are not available as business applications, planning calendars can be created and maintained using configuration activity 102629 – Define Planning Calendar. In a current 3-system landscape the configuration activity is accessed through SAP Central Business Configuration. In older 2-system landscapes, it available through Manage Your Solution. Available app is MD27 (Display Planning Calendar)

More details below:

SAP Learning – Configuring Production Planning Master Data

SAP Community – How to Create Planning Calendar in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

SAP Fiori app Display Planning Calendar

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 3 in review
entry.content:
In my Day 2 recap, I showed what the Autonomous Enterprise looks like in Finance, CX, and Supply Chain.  All of it runs on one foundation: the SAP Business AI Platform. This is where agents get built, where they get context, and where they get governed. Day 3 was for the builders: the developers, architects, and technical consultants who turn this vision into reality.

Here's my recap of the day's sessions and the questions that came up most.

The morning briefing (recording) started with a provocative question: Is AI the new plastic? AI is the most overrated and underrated technology at the same time. Some people don't use it where they should. Others use it for everything without understanding its limitations or how to design these systems well. Plastic works the same way. It's an incredible material in medicine, and completely absurd when used to wrap a banana that already has a peel.

The message for Day 3: build AI intentionally. Know where it adds real value and if you're building agents, build the evaluation framework alongside them.

This is what we then saw in the new, fully managed Joule Studio, planned for general availability in Q3 2026. It introduces intent-based development: describe what you want to accomplish in natural language and Joule Studio guides the journey from business intent to production-ready agent. For example: We process 50,000 invoices each month with a team of 10. Most delays are due to matching issues and invoices being routed to the wrong approver. I'm looking for an automated solution. With one prompt, the agent handles classification, matching, routing, and exception escalation autonomously. Rewatch Step-by-step guidance to build custom Joule agents in Joule Studio to see how.

Answers to your most asked questions:

  • When is Joule Studio GA and how do I get access?
    GA is planned for end of Q3 2026. The Early Adopter Care program has started and registration is now closed. Trial access is being planned.
  • What happens to Joule Studio Classic?
    Joule Studio Classic continues with a free promotion as part of SAP Build. A migration path to the new, fully managed SAP Joule Studio is available that automatically converts your existing agents and skills into the new format which is the recommended path.
  • Do I need a technical background to use it? The intent-based development is designed for different levels of technical experience. Joule chat guides you through the entire solution or code generation with natural language.

The invoice demo in Joule Studio was no coincidence. Unstructured documents such as invoices, contracts, delivery notes, and quality certificates are where enormous amounts of business value get stuck. SAP Document AI (recording) is built to unlock them.

Did you know about the recent and upcoming feature releases in SAP Document AI?

  • Inbound and outbound channel support, which enables you to fetch the e-mail, extract both the e-mail body and attachments, such as Excel files. Follow the documentation to register an e-mail app here.
  • Standard extraction supports most document fields. But what about the ones specific to your business? With custom prompting you can write your extraction rules in plain language, choose your model, and combine with built-in extraction in the same schema. (Planned for Q3/2026)
  • With everyone talking about agents, how can agentic AI help with document processing? Agentic workflows shift document processing from a tool that reads to an agent that can reason and act when validating a payment advice note. (Planned for Q3/2026)

Answer to your most asked question:

  • What file types and document formats are supported?
    It depends on your license. The base edition covers the most common business document types. The premium edition extends to additional formats CSV, for example, is not included in the base edition. The full list is in the SAP Help documentation.

Custom prompting in SAP Document AI already hinted at it: you choose your model, you write your rules. That flexibility raises a bigger question: how do you access, evaluate, and govern all the models available to you across your entire AI landscape? That's what the session Build Your Own AI Solution with Generative AI hub answered. Rewatch it here.

Generative AI hub gives you access to leading frontier models like GPT-5.5, Claude 4.8 Opus, Gemini 3.5 Flash, Mistral Small 4, and SAP's own foundation models. The platform is built around one core insight: the best AI is not one model. Different tasks need different intelligence. The Evaluation Service is how you find the right fit and benchmark any model against your own data and metrics. Get started by finding the right model for the right task: first, find a use case that is visible and impactful. Then experiment, start small, iterate fast, and learn what works. Finally, operationalize, build the framework, define standards, and share what you've learned across your organization. Try it yourself with the free 30-day trial or continue with the on-demand CodeJam hands-on exercise “Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core” on GitHub.

Answers to your most asked questions:

  • Where can I find the full list of supported models?
    See SAP Note 3437766 and the SAP Road Map Explorer for the latest model catalog, regional availability, and input/output token details. Use the Generative AI hub in SAP AI Core Calculator to estimate usage.
  • How is data security handled and where does my data go?
    SAP does not allow model providers to train on your data. SAP has contractual agreements with model providers (like OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, etc.) ensuring that customer prompts and completions are not used to train or fine-tune their models. All LLM calls are proxied through SAP AI Core / Generative AI hub.

One model on this platform deserves particular attention: SAP-RPT-1.5, the Relational Pre-trained Transformer. It is planned to become generally available with GenAI Hub 2607a, scheduled for July 20. The dedicated session explained why this matters: while LLMs are powerful for language-based tasks, they are not designed to work with tables. Real-world ERP, CRM, and SCM tables are typically not part of standard LLM training data. In addition, LLMs cannot reliably reason numerically across columns, which increases the risk of inaccurate or fabricated values when they are uncertain. This is unacceptable for financial and operational decisions. Rewatch SAP-RPT-1: The SAP Foundation Model for Structured Data to learn about the six adoption scenarios for SAP-RPT-1 that are planned to go live in the coming months. Most importantly, start testing with the free playground at rpt.cloud.sap.

Answer to your most asked questions:

  • When is RPT-1.5 generally available and what happens to RPT-1?
    RPT-1.5 is scheduled for GA in July 2026. RPT-1 deprecation is not yet scheduled and there will be sufficient migration time, and switching is straightforward as the APIs are almost identical.

The day closed with the very first virtual CodeJam "Build code-based AI Agents" where you learned in a training system how to build state-of-the-art AI agents using Generative AI hub, Python and LangGraph. You gained the skills to create custom tools for your agents, including leveraging SAP's RPT-1 model and deploying your agents to BTP. Rewatch Build code-based AI Agents and access all materials on GitHub to get hands on yourself anytime.

Day 1 set the foundation. Day 2 put it into practice. Day 3 gave you the tools to build it. Continue with the full series:

  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 4 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 1 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 2 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 3 in review

entry.title: Re: How to create Business Partner Master Data to S4 Hana...
entry.content:
Hi @ayechan

 

For your scenario, Master Data Governance is the source, and Public Cloud is the target. Communication arrangements are required in different systems and serve different purposes.

Question 1: Do we need to create communication arrangement for both SAP_COM_0008 (Business Partner, Customer, and Supplier Integration) and SAP_COM_0878 in s4hana public cloud tenant?

Answer: No, but rather you should go to target cloud and create your communication arrangement for SAP_COM_0008, Business Partner, Customer and Supplier Integration. This scenario exposes the inbound SOAP services through which the system receives Business Partner, Customer and Supplier data.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6ef7f849fed34f95adfc449f29835255/42d25fb7d75a484c8df17432d8cb1c9d.html?locale=en-US

Ensure you create SAP_COM_0878 in the MDG cloud, not in the target cloud. SAP_COM:0878 is the communication scenario used by MDG cloud for SOAP based master data exchange with target systems.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/mdg-ce/administration-guide/create-communication-arrangement?locale=en-US

Here is the setup for direct SOAP replication: MDG cloud SAO_COM_0878 — Cloud SAP_COM_0008

 

Question 2: If we use SAP_COM_0878, we will need to create BP data from thirdparty API tool?

Answer: No. You cannot create Business Partners through postman or another API client, but your users can create and approve the Business Partner inside MDG cloud. After the governance process is completed, MDG sends the Business Partner automatically through the configured SOAP replication channel. A third-party tool is required only when you want to create or update data of an external application.

Question 3: For now, I added SAP Master Data Governance, Cloud Edition Service only in BTP. Is it required to add additional service or instance for the case I mentioned?

Answer: For direct SOAP replication, you do not require an additional custom application or integration Suite instance. You will be needing the MDG cloud subscription, the appropriate users and roles, and the communication configuration in both systems.

Master Data Integration is an alternative replication architecture. When you select that option, Master Data Integration service instance and distribution model configuration are required. You should not configure both SOAP and Master Data Integration for the same replication flow unless your architecture explicitly requires both.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/mdg-ce/administration-guide/subscribe-to-btp-services-and-instance?locale=en-US

Question 4: Do I need to create destination in BTP that is connected to the target S4hana cloud system? And also, do I need to create communication arrangement in MDG tenant? If yes, which scenario ID will we need to use?

Answer: For direct SOAP replication, you should configure the target as a communication system in the MDG, then create the SAP_COM_0878 communication arrangement and assign it to the system.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/mdg-ce/administration-guide/how-to-create-communication-systems?locale=en-US

You can manually create a separate BTP destination not as the central configuration for this replication method. The communication arrangement contains the target endpoint and authentication information. Destinations are specifically required in certain Master Data Intergration SOAP scenarios.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/master-data-integration/sap-master-data-integration-prod/configuring-destinations-for-soap-api-in-sap-master-data-integration?locale=en-US

  

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Batch Determination Not Triggering Automatically for Subcontracting Outbound Delivery in SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Hi Team,

We are trying to implement automatic batch determination for Subcontracting Outbound Deliveries created through the Subcontracting Cockpit app in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

We have already completed the following configurations:

  • Maintained the required Batch Classes
  • Maintained Batch Search Strategy Records
  • Configured the Condition Tables
  • Maintained the Sort Sequence
  • Assigned the necessary batch determination settings

However, batch determination is still not being triggered automatically in the subcontracting outbound delivery. The system is not proposing any batch even though batch stock is available and the configuration appears to be in place.

Could you please advise on:

  • Any additional configuration required specifically for subcontracting outbound deliveries?
  • Whether batch determination is supported for deliveries created through the Subcontracting Cockpit app in Public Cloud?
  • Any prerequisites or known limitations that need to be considered?

For reference, we have also reviewed the batch determination log, which indicates that no valid search strategy is being found and the available batches do not correspond to the selection criteria.

Your guidance on resolving this issue would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks & Regards,
Kiran

entry.title: UI5 Linter in Action: A Hands-On Workshop at UI5con 2026
entry.content:
UI5 Linter in Action: A Hands-On Workshop at UI5con 2026

Still running legacy OpenUI5/SAPUI5 code with deprecated APIs, inline scripts, and global variables? It's time to modernize — and this workshop shows you exactly how.

What Happened

At UI5con 2026 Germany, we ran a one-hour hands-on workshop where attendees took a real OpenUI5/SAPUI5 application from "legacy" to "future-proof" using UI5 linter. Step by step, participants:

  • Ran their first UI5 linter report and learned what it tells them
  • Auto-fixed issues with a single command
  • Manually resolved deprecated APIs, CSP violations, and legacy patterns
  • Integrated UI5 linter into a CI pipeline so regressions never come back

The feedback was fantastic — attendees loved the practical, hands-on format and walked away with real skills they could immediately apply to their own projects.

Do It Yourself

Couldn't make it to UI5con? No problem. This workshop is fully self-paced and available for you to complete on your own. All you need:

Prerequisites

  • Node.js (LTS) and Git installed
  • A code editor of your choice
  • Basic UI5 development experience

Simply clone the workshop repository and follow the step-by-step instructions. You'll get the same experience as the live attendees — modernizing a real app from start to finish at your own pace.

What You'll Learn

  • How the UI5 linter identifies deprecated APIs, CSP violations, and legacy patterns
  • How to auto-fix common issues with a single command
  • How to manually resolve findings that require deeper changes
  • How to integrate UI5 linter into your CI pipeline for ongoing code quality

Bonus: AI-Assisted Modernization

As an outlook, the workshop briefly explores the UI5 Modernization plugin for coding agents — automating the very steps you'll learn manually.

Clone the repo, follow the steps, and modernize your first app today!

entry.title: SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 4 in review
entry.content:
Day 4 shifted the focus from individual capabilities to enterprise-wide readiness. After three days of product deep-dives and hands-on exploration, the final day stepped back to ask the harder questions: How does all of this fit together architecturally? How do you govern it? And what does it actually take to make AI work at enterprise scale, not in a proof of concept, but across an entire organization? Four sessions structured the day around architecture, trust, and long-term readiness.

Here's my recap of the day's sessions and the questions that came up most.

In my Day 2 recap, we already explored why AI initiatives are stuck in pilot mode. They often lack business context, while governance requirements can slow deployment. Day 4’s opening session returned to this theme and explored it in more depth. SAP's response is built-in responsibility structured around three components designed into the platform from the start:

  • AI Ethics & Safety defines what AI should and should not do;
  • AI Security protects systems and data from model-layer risks;
  • AI Compliance stays aligned with regulatory obligations.

Defining what responsible AI means is the straightforward part. Operationalizing it at enterprise scale, across multi-cloud environments, with real compliance obligations is the hard part. Four considerations stood out: AI needs business-ready data; responsible AI must be built in from the start; AI must be deployed with strong safeguards such as grounding, masking, prompt templates, and access controls; and governance must continue across the full AI lifecycle, especially for agents.

With the governance framework established, the second session zoomed out further to explain how SAP is rethinking the entire enterprise software stack. In simple terms, this means SAP is redesigning the enterprise stack so agents can understand business context, act across processes, and deliver outcomes through Joule:

  • Platform Layer: Agent lifecycle, identity, routing, and governance built in
  • Foundation Layer: Moving from a "system of record" to a "system of context", data that's semantically grounded and traceable
  • Process Layer: Apps become capability providers for agents, not just containers for rules
  • UX Layer: Joule as the primary entry point; intent-driven, proactive, outcome-focused

Answers to your most asked question:

  • What is the difference between Knowledge Core and Knowledge Graph?
    The Knowledge Core is the broader concept, the layer where SAP brings together semantically rich, business-meaningful data from across the enterprise. The Knowledge Graph is a component within the Knowledge Core that defines the semantic relationships and context between data entities (e.g., how a Customer relates to an Order, a Contract, or a Product).
  • Can customers access or view the Knowledge Graph?
    No, not directly. SAP can see and work with the Knowledge Graph, but it is treated as SAP intellectual property ("the secret sauce"). It is not exposed to customers or partners as a browsable artefact. How do you use it if you can't see it? You consume its output. The Knowledge Graph enriches the context that Joule and SAP Business AI use to answer questions, make recommendations, and reason over business data. You benefit from it indirectly every time Joule provides contextually grounded answers.
  • Does SAP Knowledge Graph work with non-SAP data?
    Yes. If non-SAP data is connected or combined in SAP Business Data Cloud, the Knowledge Graph can operate over it.
  • How does A2A work and how can standard SAP agents connect with custom agents built in Joule Studio?
    A2A is an open protocol, and SAP is a founding member that actively contributes to its development. Because it is publicly available, developers can understand how it works and use it across different frameworks. This also reflects one of SAP’s architecture principles: leveraging open-source capabilities where they add value. For example, agents built with frameworks such as LangGraph can use A2A to connect with other agents. Similarly, an agent developed in Joule Studio is deployed to the SAP Agent Runtime and supports A2A, allowing Joule and other agents to interact with it in a standard, interoperable way.

To go deeper, rewatch the sessions on operationalizing responsible AI in SAP and understanding what is changing in enterprise software:

  1. Operationalizing Responsible AI in SAP: Data Readiness, Secure Deployment & Interoperable Governance (recording)
  2. Inside the Business AI Mind: Four Building Blocks of SAP's New Stack (recording)

The previous session covered what SAP is building architecturally, this one focused on how Joule is specifically engineered to make it real.

Today, different Joule deployments across SAP solutions can feel inconsistent with different identities, different setups, different capabilities. The goal is simple to state, complex to deliver: a unified Joule experience. That means:

  • One identity and access management layer (via SAP Cloud Identity Services)
  • User accounts consolidated across all SAP solutions
  • Every Joule interaction happens in the context of you — your role, your authorizations, your audit trail

The Big Shift: From Customer-Managed to SAP-Managed Joule

Right now, deploying Joule is a heavy lift for customers, a manual setup, many dependencies, activation projects that can take months. SAP is changing this fundamentally. Once generally available, you can activate Joule in SAP for Me with lightweight setup; SAP manages tenants and the integration lifecycle, giving you continuous access to new Joule innovations as they are delivered.

If you're currently running a Joule implementation project, you don't need to stop. SAP-managed Joule will become the default activation path going forward, and migration paths will be available. Rewatch Architecting SAP Joule: Unified, Scalable, and Future Ready for further details.

The final session (recording) answered the next logical question: how do you actually get AI to scale across an enterprise?

Building on the mindset introduced in the earlier Fireside Chat: From Exploration to Enterprise Value - Lessons from Early SAP Business AI Adopters, this session brought the principles to life with a concrete example. Cirque du Soleil manages 38 shows worldwide, with over 70,000 supplier invoices per year. Their accounts payable team was overwhelmed from manually researching invoice statuses, tracking down information across systems, and drafting bilingual email responses to suppliers in French and English.

This is a good example to keep in mind when thinking about your own use cases. Not every AI win needs to be transformational on day one. Automating a manual, repetitive, frustrating manual process for a specific team is a real and measurable win. And as Cirque du Soleil shows, you don't have to wait for a grand transformation to start seeing value. Pick a painful process, apply the right tools, and measure what changes. Practical starting points include:

  • Activate AI in SAP for Me with what is already available in your landscape
  • Browse available AI capabilities across SAP solutions in the Feature Catalog. See how: 

     

  • Ask your account team for a SAP AI Discovery Workshop, a structured session to help identify your best AI use cases

For partners, SAP offers the SAP Business AI Partner-Led Adoption Incentive designed to help customers realize value from their investment by increasing SAP Business AI Platform and agent consumption through the SAP Partner ecosystem. Find more information on the SAP Partner Portal Page.

Day 1 set the foundation. Day 2 showed what SAP Business AI looks like in practice. Day 3 gave you the tools to start building. Day 4 brought the bigger picture together: responsible AI, trusted business context, scalable architecture, and practical paths to adoption.

The week may be over, but this is where the real work begins. The next step is to turn what you learned into concrete action: explore what is already available in your SAP landscape, identify the processes where AI can remove real friction, and keep building your skills through the learning resources below. AI adoption does not start with a grand transformation. It starts with one well-chosen use case, the right governance, and the confidence to move from learning to doing.

To support that next step, here are the learning formats and resources to continue building your SAP AI skills.

  • Beyond Hype live sessions: Join practical sessions for Joule practitioners and AI Platform developers who want to move from concepts to productive use.
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills Monthly Blog Series: Stay up to date on new AI learning assets, sessions, and learning opportunities.
  • Artificial Intelligence at SAP Learning: Build structured, skill-based knowledge through guided learning journeys, expert-led sessions, and certifications.
  • SAP CodeJam sessions: Join in person hands-on developer events in your city to apply SAP Business AI concepts in practice.

Full series:

  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 1 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 2 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 3 in review
  • SAP AI Adoption & Learning Week – Day 4 in review

entry.title: Re: SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 Beta Program – Program finished (update from 28th of November 2025)
entry.content:
Hi @alexandrepoitras ,
I apologize for all the issues which occurred during releasing the 8.10 Compilation 1 media.
My colleagues from the Assembly team have just informed me that the issues (export control, name of the file, size of the download, folder name inside the archive) should now be resolved. From SAP GUI for Windows perspective, the content of the download was correct all the time (assuming that you could download it).
Thank you for your patience.
Best regards,
Frank
